critic_raw:
Three critical evaluation gaps for the ceramic defect classifier:

1. (accuracy_hides_rare_defect_performance) 1.4% defect prevalence means 98.6% of images are defect-free. A model predicting all images as defect-free achieves 98.6% accuracy — essentially the baseline the paper reports. The 0.5 pp improvement from the new model tells us nothing about whether the model detects any defects at all. At this prevalence, accuracy is a proxy for non-defect classification, not defect detection.

2. (metric_not_aligned_with_asymmetric_costs) The paper states escaped defects are far more costly than false rejections. Yet the evaluation uses a symmetric metric (overall accuracy). This is a direct contradiction between the stated evaluation objective (minimize escapes) and the reported metric (minimize total misclassifications equally).

3. (operating_point_omitted) No recall on the defect class, no precision at a fixed review threshold, no false negatives per million parts. The business decision is 'will this model reduce escapes?' — and none of the reported metrics answer this question.
defender_raw:
The evaluation demonstrates:
- 99.1% accuracy on a held-out test set with 240,000 training images
- Five random seeds producing consistent results
- Operational viability at 45 fps on edge hardware
- Sound preprocessing (brightness normalization, geometric augmentation)

The model is ready for deployment evaluation on the line.
adjudication_raw:
The defender provides no substantive rebuttal. The accuracy figure (99.1%) does not address whether defects are being detected because at 1.4% prevalence, this number is dominated by the non-defect class. The random-seeds result confirms training stability, not that the model has acceptable defect recall. The operational viability metrics (45 fps, edge device) are separate from metric validity.

The three issues — accuracy masking at low prevalence, cost-asymmetry not reflected in the metric, operating-point omission — are all unrebutted. There is no defense of the metric choice or any argument that overall accuracy is sufficient for this use case.

Verdict: critique_wins
